planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; hairy alumroot is the most heat-and-humidity-tolerant native Heuchera species and outperforms H. americana and H. sanguinea cultivars in zone 7–9 summer heat.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; hairy alumroot is the native Heuchera of the Southern Appalachians and Ozarks and is significantly more tolerant of heat and humidity than other native Heuchera species — making it the top choice for shade gardens in zone 7–9 where summer temperatures are brutal. The large, maple-shaped leaves with dense soft hairs (giving a velvety texture) are handsome throughout the season, and small white flowers on 2–3 foot stems appear in late summer when little else in the shade garden is blooming. This species is the parent of many popular hybrid 'coral bells' cultivars, including 'Autumn Bride' (exceptionally heat-tolerant with large white flowers in fall). The foliage is semi-evergreen in zone 7–8 and provides attractive winter interest. Divide every 3–4 years as the crown heaves upward from the soil.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; American alumroot is the most widespread native Heuchera species and the parent of many ornamental cultivars, though H. villosa outperforms it in hot, humid climates of zone 7–9.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade; American alumroot is the most widely distributed native Heuchera of eastern North America, found on rocky woodland slopes, cliff faces, and shaded stream banks from Canada to Georgia. The attractive, rounded, shallowly lobed leaves are often marked with attractive metallic silver marbling and remain semi-evergreen in mild winters, providing year-round ground-level interest in shaded borders. Small greenish-white flowers on 18–24 inch stems in late spring are modest but attract native bees. The species is one of the important parents in the development of the hugely popular hybrid coral bells (H. × hybrida) industry, contributing foliage coloring genes. Grows in average to poor soils with good drainage — excessive fertility and moisture promote crown rot. Divide every 3–4 years when the crown heaves from soil.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; in zone 8 the stems freeze to the ground in hard winters but resprout vigorously from the deep, heat-tolerant root system each spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; coral bean is one of the most striking native shrubs of the Gulf Coast and Southeast, producing brilliant scarlet tubular flowers in long spikes in spring before the leaves emerge — they are among the most important hummingbird nectar plants in the Southeast. In fall the pods split open to reveal vivid coral-red seeds that are highly ornamental but extremely toxic if swallowed. The stems bear recurved spines that deter browsing; the plant dies to the ground in hard freezes in zone 7–8 but regrows rapidly from an established root crown. It tolerates partial shade, sandy soil, drought, and salt spray — making it an outstanding native plant for difficult spots in Gulf Coast gardens. The red seeds were traditionally used as ornamentation and have important Native American cultural uses.

planting.note: Surface sow seed in fall in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; seeds require cold stratification so fall sowing or winter sowing is preferred. Giant ironweed naturalizes readily in moist soils and can be aggressive — allow 4–6 feet of space.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; giant ironweed is one of the most dramatic native wildflowers for the back of a large border or naturalized area, growing 5–10 feet tall with flat-topped clusters of intense royal purple flowers in late summer and fall that are irresistible to monarchs, painted ladies, giant swallowtails, and dozens of native bee species. The vivid purple color is one of the most saturated of any native wildflower. It grows naturally along stream banks, moist woodland edges, and roadside ditches throughout the eastern US and is one of the most reliable and showy native plants for late-season pollinator value. The tall stems and architectural seed heads provide winter interest and bird food. It spreads by rhizome in moist soil and should be given space or divided annually to control spread in the border.

planting.note: Plant acorns immediately upon collection (they cannot be stored dry) in fall in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade; white oak develops a very deep taproot and should be planted small and allowed to establish — transplanting large trees is difficult.

directions: Plant fresh acorns in fall or container-grown small trees in spring in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade; white oak is arguably the most ecologically important tree species in eastern North America, supporting more species of caterpillars (534 species of moths and butterflies) than any other native tree genus — a keystone species for the entire eastern food web. Individual trees live 300–600 years and develop into majestic, wide-spreading specimens with deeply furrowed ash-white bark and a broad, irregular crown. The sweet acorns (low tannin, suitable for direct consumption by wildlife and humans) ripen annually and provide critical food for deer, turkeys, wood ducks, blue jays, and dozens of other species. The spectacular crimson, orange, and burgundy fall color rivals the maples. Plant from acorn when possible; the taproot develops early and transplanting large specimens is extremely difficult. A tree for generational thinking.
